allopack.title: Photosynthesis — How Plants Eat Sunlight
allopack.author: AlloFlow community catalog (authored by Claude, reviewed by A. Pomeranz)
allopack.standards: NGSS MS-LS1-6 (construct an explanation for how photosynthesis cycles matter and flows energy); NGSS MS-LS1-7 (food rearranged into new molecules that support growth); CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RST.6-8.4 (domain-specific vocabulary)

data.body:
**Due:** (your teacher will tell you)

Every bite of food you have ever eaten traces back to a plant catching sunlight. Today you find out exactly how that trick works — and why you are breathing because of it.

1. Read **The Sugar Factory in Every Leaf**. Notice the three ingredients and the two products.
2. Open the **Glossary** and win the **Word Scramble** — these words show up in everything else.
3. Walk through **Follow One Carbon Atom** to see the order things happen.
4. Study the **Inputs and Outputs Map**, then try the sort: does each card go IN or OUT?
5. Take the quiz. It hunts for the two ideas almost everyone gets backwards.
6. Write your explanation with the **Sentence Frames**.

One warning: two of the quiz questions are traps that catch even adults. Read carefully. 🌱☀️

objectives.label: I explained photosynthesis to someone without reading from the screen

history.title: The Sugar Factory in Every Leaf
history.data:
## The Sugar Factory in Every Leaf

A tree standing in a field looks like it is doing nothing. It is actually running one of the most important chemical factories on Earth, and it is running on sunlight.

Here is the puzzle that confused scientists for centuries: where does the *stuff* of a tree come from? A giant oak weighs many tons. Does it eat the soil? In the 1600s, a scientist named Jan Baptist van Helmont grew a willow in a pot for five years. The tree gained about 74 kilograms. The soil lost almost nothing. The mass had come from somewhere else.

Most of it came from the **air**.

Inside every leaf are tiny green structures called **chloroplasts**, packed with a pigment called **chlorophyll**. Chlorophyll absorbs red and blue light and reflects green — which is the only reason leaves look green to you. That captured light energy powers a chemical reaction with three ingredients: **carbon dioxide** from the air, **water** from the roots, and light itself.

The reaction rearranges those atoms into two new things: **glucose**, a sugar the plant uses for food and building material, and **oxygen**, which leaves through the same tiny pores that let carbon dioxide in. Those pores are called **stomata**, and a plant opens and closes them like windows — wide open to feed, squeezed shut when water is scarce.

So the oak tree is built, quite literally, out of thin air and water, assembled by sunlight.

Now the part that surprises people: plants also perform **cellular respiration**, the same process your cells use to release energy from sugar. Plants do it day and night. Photosynthesis is not the opposite of breathing — it is how the plant makes the fuel that its breathing burns. During bright daylight a healthy plant photosynthesizes much faster than it respires, so oxygen flows out on balance.

Every breath you take contains oxygen released by this reaction. Every calorie you eat, whether from broccoli or a burger, was originally sunlight captured by chlorophyll. Trace any food chain backward far enough and it always ends at a leaf, quietly turning light into life.

data.def: The process plants use to turn light energy, carbon dioxide, and water into sugar and oxygen.
The green pigment inside leaves that absorbs light energy.
The tiny structure inside a plant cell where photosynthesis happens.
The sugar a plant makes and uses for energy and for building itself.
Tiny pores on a leaf that let gases in and out.
How living cells release energy from sugar — plants do this too, day and night.
A substance that absorbs some colors of light and reflects others.
An ingredient that goes INTO a chemical reaction.
Something that comes OUT of a chemical reaction.
The ability to make something happen or change — sunlight carries it to the leaf.

data.progressionLabel: PROCESS: Carbon enters the leaf (Step 1) → Carbon becomes part of you (Step 7)

items.event: A carbon dioxide molecule drifts through the air and reaches a leaf surface.
The molecule passes through an open stoma into the inside of the leaf.
Inside a chloroplast, chlorophyll absorbs red and blue light energy from the Sun.
That captured energy powers a reaction between the carbon dioxide and water drawn up from the roots.
The atoms rearrange: a glucose molecule is assembled, and oxygen atoms are left over.
Oxygen exits through the stomata into the air, and the glucose is stored or used to build plant tissue.
An animal eats the plant, and that same carbon atom becomes part of the animal's body.

branches.title: Reactants (what goes IN)
items: Carbon dioxide — from the air, through the stomata
Water — from the soil, up through the roots and stem

branches.title: Where it happens
items: Inside chloroplasts, which hold the chlorophyll

branches.title: Products (what comes OUT)
items: Glucose — food and building material for the plant
Oxygen — released through the stomata into the air

branches.title: Why it matters
items: Nearly all food chains begin here
The oxygen you breathe came from this reaction
Plant mass comes mostly from air, not soil

history.title: In, Out, or Neither?

categories.label: Place or part, not an ingredient

history.meta: 3 categories • 9 cards • soil card is the trap

questions.question: A 500-kilogram oak tree grew from a tiny acorn. Where did most of that mass come from?
options: Carbon dioxide from the air
Minerals and soil the roots ate
Water alone
Sunlight turning directly into wood

questions.question: Why do leaves look green?
options: Chlorophyll reflects green light instead of absorbing it
Chlorophyll absorbs green light most strongly
Green is the color of glucose inside the leaf
Leaves are dyed green by minerals from the soil
questions.correctAnswer: Chlorophyll reflects green light instead of absorbing it

questions.question: Do plants perform cellular respiration?
options: Yes — day and night, using the sugar they made
No — respiration is only for animals
Only at night, when photosynthesis stops
Only when they are sick or dying
questions.correctAnswer: Yes — day and night, using the sugar they made

questions.question: Which list shows ONLY reactants of photosynthesis?
options: Carbon dioxide, water, light energy
Glucose, oxygen, water
Carbon dioxide, glucose, chlorophyll
Oxygen, light energy, soil minerals

questions.question: A plant is kept in a dark closet with plenty of water. What happens to photosynthesis?
options: It stops, because light energy is a required ingredient
It continues at the same rate using stored sugar
It speeds up, because the plant is trying harder
It switches to using soil minerals instead of light
questions.correctAnswer: It stops, because light energy is a required ingredient

questions.question: Van Helmont's willow gained 74 kg while the soil lost almost none. Explain what his experiment proved and what it did NOT prove.
questions.expectedAnswer: It proved the tree's mass did not come from soil. It did not prove where the mass DID come from — he thought it was water alone, but most of it actually came from carbon dioxide in the air.

questions.question: Explain why photosynthesis matters to an animal that never eats plants — like a wolf.
questions.expectedAnswer: The wolf eats animals that ate plants, so the energy and carbon in its food originally came from photosynthesis. The wolf also breathes oxygen released by photosynthesis.

reflections.text: Which idea in this lesson was different from what you believed before? Describe what you used to think.
If you could run van Helmont's willow experiment again with modern tools, what would you measure that he could not?

items.text: The two products of photosynthesis are ____ and ____.
Leaves look green because chlorophyll ____ green light instead of ____ it.
Most of a tree's mass comes from ____, not from ____, which we know because ____.
Plants perform respiration ____, which means photosynthesis is not the opposite of breathing but rather ____.
A wolf depends on photosynthesis because ____.
One thing I still wonder about photosynthesis is ____.

data.rubric:
| Criteria | 1 — Starting | 3 — Getting There | 5 — Scientist |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vocabulary | Everyday words only | 2-3 glossary terms used correctly | 4+ terms used correctly and precisely |
| Causal reasoning | States facts separately | Connects two ideas with because | Builds a full chain: ingredient to product to consequence |
| Accuracy | A major misconception remains | Mostly accurate, one slip | Every claim scientifically correct |

data.question: If plants make oxygen, do they suffocate at night? 🌙
data.answer: No — at night they stop photosynthesizing but keep respiring, so they take in a little oxygen and release a little carbon dioxide. The amounts are tiny; a bedroom plant will not steal your air.

data.question: Why are some leaves red or purple then? 🍁
data.answer: Those leaves have other pigments (like anthocyanins) layered over the chlorophyll. The chlorophyll is usually still there doing its job underneath.

data.question: Could a plant live on a colored light — like only red?
data.answer: Yes, surprisingly well. Chlorophyll absorbs red and blue best, so red light works. Pure green light is the worst choice, because that is the color chlorophyll reflects away.

data.question: Do plants in the ocean matter?
data.answer: Enormously. Tiny floating algae called phytoplankton run photosynthesis at massive scale and produce a huge share of Earth's oxygen — arguably more than rainforests.

data.question: Is it true a plant is basically made of air? 💨
data.answer: Mostly yes, and it sounds impossible until you know carbon dioxide is a gas made of solid-forming atoms. The carbon in wood was floating in the sky not long ago.

data.question: Why can't we just build machines that do photosynthesis?
data.answer: Scientists are trying — it is called artificial photosynthesis. Copying what a leaf does cheaply and at scale turns out to be extremely hard, which is a good measure of how impressive leaves are.
